title: Cromwell
content:
آ آ آ آ They took dead Cromwell from his grave,
آ آ آ آ And stuck his head on high;
آ آ آ آ The Merry Monarch and his men,
آ آ آ آ They laughed as they passed by
آ آ آ آ The common people cheered and jeered,
آ آ آ آ To Englandï؟½s deep disgrace,
آ آ آ آ The crowds whoï؟½d neï؟½er have dared to look
آ آ آ آ Live Cromwell in the face.

آ آ آ آ He came in Englandï؟½s direst need
آ آ آ آ With law and fire and sword,
آ آ آ آ He thrashed her enemies at home
آ آ آ آ And crushed her foes abroad;
آ آ آ آ He kept his word by sea and land,
آ آ آ آ His parliament he schooled,
آ آ آ آ He made the nations understand
آ آ آ آ A Man in England ruled!

آ آ آ آ Van Tromp, with twice the English ships,
آ آ آ آ And flushed by victory,
آ آ آ آ A great broom to his masthead bound,
آ آ آ آ Set sail to sweep the sea.
آ آ آ آ But Englandï؟½s ruler was a man
آ آ آ آ Who needed lots of room,
آ آ آ آ So Blake soon lowered the Dutchmanï؟½s tone,
آ آ آ آ And smashed the Dutchmanï؟½s broom.

آ آ آ آ He sent a bill to Tuscany
آ آ آ آ For sixty thousand pounds,
آ آ آ آ For wrong done to his subjects there,
آ آ آ آ And merchants in her bounds.
آ آ آ آ He sent by Debt Collector Blake,
آ آ آ آ And, you need but be told
آ آ آ آ That, by the Duke of Tuscany
آ آ آ آ That bill was paid in gold.

آ آ آ آ To pirate ports in Africa
آ آ آ آ He sent a message grim
آ آ آ آ To have each captured Englishman
آ آ آ آ Delivered up to him;
آ آ آ آ And every ship and cargoï؟½s worth,
آ آ آ آ And every boat and gun,
آ آ آ آ And this, all this, as Dickens says,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Was gloriously done.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½d tortured English prisoners
آ آ آ آ Whoï؟½d sailed the Spanish Main;
آ آ آ آ So Cromwell sent a little bill
آ آ آ آ By Admiral Blake to Spain.
آ آ آ آ To keep his hand in, by the way.
آ آ آ آ He whipped the Portuguese;
آ آ آ آ And he made it safe for English ships
آ آ آ آ To sail the Spanish seas.

آ آ آ آ The Protestants in Southern lands
آ آ آ آ Had long been sore oppressed;
آ آ آ آ They sent their earnest prayers to Noll
آ آ آ آ To have their wrongs redressed.
آ آ آ آ He sent a message to the Powers,
آ آ آ آ In which he told them flat,
آ آ آ آ All men must praise God as they chose,
آ آ آ آ Or he would see to that.

آ آ آ آ And, when heï؟½d hanged the fools at home
آ آ آ آ And settled foreign rows,
آ آ آ آ He found the time to potter round
آ آ آ آ Amongst his pigs and cows.
آ آ آ آ Of private rows he never spoke,
آ آ آ آ That grand old Ironsides.
آ آ آ آ They said a fatherï؟½s strong heart broke
آ آ آ آ When Cromwellï؟½s daughter died.

آ آ آ آ (They dragged his body from its grave,
آ آ آ آ His head stuck on a pole,
آ آ آ آ They threw his wifeï؟½s and daughterï؟½s bones
آ آ آ آ Into a rubbish hole
آ آ آ آ To rot with those of two whoï؟½d lived
آ آ آ آ And fought for Englandï؟½s sake,
آ آ آ آ And each one in his own brave way,
آ آ آ آ Great Pym, and Admiral Blake.)

آ آ آ آ From Charles to Charles, throughout the world
آ آ آ آ Old Englandï؟½s name was high,
آ آ آ آ And thatï؟½s a thing no Royalist
آ آ آ آ Could ever yet deny.
آ آ آ آ Long shameful years have passed since then,
آ آ آ آ In spite of Englandï؟½s boast,
آ آ آ آ But Englishmen were Englishmen,
آ آ آ آ While Cromwell carved the roast.

آ آ آ آ And, in my countryï؟½s hour of need,
آ آ آ آ For it shall surely come,
آ آ آ آ While run by fools whoï؟½ll never heed
آ آ آ آ The beating of the drum.
آ آ آ آ While baffled by the fools at home,
آ آ آ آ And threatened from the sea,
آ آ آ آ Lord! send a man like Oliver,
آ آ آ آ And let me live to see.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
